footer.intro: A guide to space: ISS, asteroids, meteors, space weather and rocket launches — live.

title.home: OrbitLight — space live: ISS, asteroids, meteors, launches

desc.home: Space live in English: track the ISS, near-Earth asteroids, meteor showers, space weather and rocket launches — all on OrbitLight.
desc.iss: ISS live: ground-track map, visible passes over your city, current crew and expedition.
desc.satellites: Satellites on a real-time map: Starlink, ISS, weather and amateur orbits you can track.
desc.weather: Space weather live: Kp index, solar wind, Bz, X-ray flux, aurora forecast and OVATION map.
desc.sky: Sky for observers: visible planets, Moon phase, meteors and ISS passes for your location.
desc.meteors: Meteor showers: calendar, peak dates, ZHR, radiants and best viewing times all year round.
desc.asteroids: Asteroids: upcoming Earth close approaches, LD distances, hazardous objects and monitoring.
desc.events: Astronomy events: eclipses, conjunctions, Moon phases, retrograde stations — full calendar.
desc.launches: Rocket launches: schedule, missions, launch vehicles and livestreams of upcoming flights.
desc.deep: Deep space: space debris, gamma-ray bursts, Voyager and the latest news from beyond Earth orbit.
desc.voyager: Voyager 1 & 2: current distance, speed and light-time from Earth, updated in real time.
desc.comets: Comets: orbits, current positions, upcoming approaches and telescope/naked-eye visibility.
desc.exoplanets: Exoplanets: the latest TESS discoveries, sizes and orbits of planets around other stars, and the habitable zone.
desc.rtl: Radio astronomy: meteor pings, Jupiter and solar spectra received on an SDR receiver.
desc.community: OrbitLight community: forum, photo gallery, observation diaries and local groups.
desc.404: Page not found — return to the OrbitLight home page to see the sky now.

picker.title: Choose a city
picker.detectFail: Couldn't detect your location. Allow location access or type a city manually.

hero.title: Sky for the observer
hero.sub: Visible planets, Moon phase, space weather and tips — everything to plan a successful night of observing over your city.

moon.title: Moon phase
moon.darkNote: Ideal conditions for observing faint objects
moon.brightNote: Bright Moon interferes with faint objects

s2.title: How to prepare for the night

tips.t1_body: Get away from light pollution — 20–30 km from the city you can see twice as many stars.
tips.t2_body: A red flashlight doesn't ruin night vision — use it for charts and instruments.
tips.t3_body: Enough to see the Moon, Jupiter, the Orion nebula and the Milky Way.
tips.t4_body: Cloud cover, Moon phase and ISS passes — all in the “Sky now” dashboard.

calendar.title: Week's events
calendar.sub: ISS passes, launches and Moon phases — live.

weather.auroraDefault: Possible in the north of the country

hero.title: Space weather
hero.sub: Solar activity, geomagnetic storms and the radiation environment near Earth in real time — with charts and an aurora forecast for your latitude.

storm.body: Kp ≥ 5 — possible radio disruption and aurora south of usual latitudes.

s1.title: Kp index
s1.sub: The planetary K-index (Kp) measures Earth's magnetic-field disturbance. Values ≥ 5 indicate a geomagnetic storm (G1–G5 scale).

s2.title: Plasma stream from the Sun
s2.sub: Solar-wind speed (DSCOVR/ACE) and the Bz component of the magnetic field. Southward Bz (< 0) «opens» the magnetosphere to aurora.

s3.title: X-ray flux (GOES)
s3.sub: Soft X-ray flux 0.1–0.8 nm. Classes: A < B < C < M < X (each step ×10). M- and X-class flares affect radio communications.

s4.title: Aurora forecast
s4.sub: NOAA OVATION aurora visibility map for the Northern Hemisphere — refreshed about every 5 minutes. The aurora chance for your latitude is computed from the current Kp.
s4.tip: The aurora is visible at Kp ≥ 6 in northern Ukraine and ≥ 8 across the whole country. Watch the northern horizon as far from city lights as possible.

s5.title: Weather on Mars
s5.sub: Latest telemetry from the InSight (NASA) weather station on the surface of Mars — temperature, pressure and wind over the last sol (Martian day).

mars.title: Weather on Mars
mars.unavailable: InSight weather-station data is no longer updated (mission ended in 2022). The last available sol is shown if the feed responds.
mars.whatBody: A sol is a Martian day (24 h 39 min). InSight measured temperature and pressure in Elysium crater for over two Martian years. The mission ended in December 2022 — the feed returns the last available sol.

hero.sub: ISS, asteroids, meteors, space weather and rocket launches — the whole sky live in one place.

tonight.title: Highlights over {{city}}

hero.title: ISS & satellites
hero.sub: Track the International Space Station in real time and plan observations of its passes over your city.

map.note: TLE propagation in the browser — position updates every second.

s2.title: Brightest visible passes
s2.sub: Upcoming days. Times in UTC+3. Brightness by stellar magnitude (smaller number = brighter).

tips.t1_title: Step out 5 min before the pass
tips.t1_body: Give your eyes 10–15 min to adapt to the dark — you'll see much more.
tips.t2_body: The ISS looks like a very bright star slowly moving across the sky.
tips.t3_body: Smartphone on a tripod, 10–30 s exposure — you'll get a bright streak of the pass.

crew.title: Astronauts aboard
crew.sub: Who is currently working aboard the International Space Station — expedition members grouped by their arrival spacecraft.
crew.unavailable: Crew roster is unavailable right now. Try refreshing the page.

launches.title: Rocket launches
launches.desc: Track upcoming launches, enable reminders in the bot and watch live streams — all in one place.

archive.a1desc: First full landing of both stages within a single mission.
archive.a2desc: Confirming the rocket is ready for regular commercial missions.
archive.a3title: Electron — payload lost on second stage
archive.a3desc: Rocket Lab opened an investigation; upcoming launches temporarily paused.

hero.title: Meteor shower over your head
hero.sub: The year's most active meteor showers — peak dates, expected zenithal hourly rate (ZHR), radiant and best observing time. Active showers are highlighted live.

s1.title: Showers of this and next season
s1.sub: Showers are sorted by proximity to peak. ZHR is the zenithal hourly rate: how many meteors an ideal observer would see per hour under a clear, dark sky.

s2.title: How to watch a meteor shower

tips.t1_title: Look a bit off the radiant
tips.t1_body: Meteors are visible across the whole sky, but near the radiant their “trails” are shortest — better to look 30–60° away from it.
tips.t2_body: Get 20–30 km out of the city. Faint meteors vanish in light pollution first.
tips.t3_body: New Moon is ideal. A full Moon “washes out” everything but the brightest fireballs. The “For observer” page shows the phase.
tips.t4_title: Put the phone away and wait
tips.t4_body: Eyes need 20 minutes to adapt. Lie back in a chair, grab a blanket and count for at least an hour — ZHR is an estimate.

hero.title: Astronomical events
hero.sub: A calendar of eclipses, planetary conjunctions, supermoons and retrograde motions — a year ahead. Plus a concise digest of this week's events: what you can already see tonight.

next.label: Eclipse
next.title: Next eclipse
next.fallback: No eclipses expected in the coming year.

what.title: What is an eclipse?
what.body: A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon covers the Sun; a lunar eclipse when Earth casts its shadow on the Moon. A total solar eclipse is one of the most awe-inspiring celestial sights, but it is only visible from a narrow strip on Earth.

s1.title: Eclipses and planetary conjunctions
s1.sub: Events of the coming year — eclipses (solar and lunar) and planetary conjunctions, when two bodies appear close together in the sky.

s2.title: Events of the next 7 days
s2.sub: Digest: meteor-shower peaks, Moon phases (including supermoon), conjunctions, eclipses and planetary retrograde motions over the next seven days — computed with skyfield ephemerides.

events.supermoonBody: — a full Moon near perigee (closest to Earth), appears 14% larger and brighter.
events.retroBody: — a planet appears to move “backward” among the stars: an optical effect of Earth overtaking it in its orbit.

hero.title: Asteroids approaching Earth
hero.sub: Upcoming asteroid close approaches with Earth. Distance is shown in “lunar” units (LD) — distances to the Moon.

orbitMap.title: Near-Earth asteroid orbits
orbitMap.head: Near-Earth asteroids
orbitMap.speed: Body positions as of now, computed from NASA orbital elements

hero.title:
Icy wanderers
from the edges of the system
hero.sub: Where comets are now on their orbit, when perihelion arrives and how bright they can be seen from Earth.

comets.coordsFallback: {{sun}} AU to the Sun · {{earth}} AU to Earth · perihelion in {{peri}} days
comets.brightnessNote: Weeks before/after perihelion · lower on the chart = brighter
comets.readBody: The magnitude drops closer to perihelion — the comet is brightest when closest to the Sun, then gradually fades as it moves away.

famous.f1_body: The most famous periodic comet in history; last seen in 1986.
famous.f2_body: Visible to the naked eye for 18 consecutive months — a record among recent comets.
famous.f3_body: The brightest comet for the Northern Hemisphere in 23 years, visible even from cities.

howto.h1_body: Even a bright comet is lost in city light pollution — get out of town or to a park far from streetlights.
howto.h2_body: The tail and coma open up better in 7×50 or 10×50 binoculars than in the narrow field of a telescope.
howto.h3_body: A bright Moon washes out a faint comet tail — pick days near new Moon.

hero.title:
Worlds around
other stars
hero.sub: The TESS satellite finds new exoplanet candidates every day. OrbitLight shows the freshest discoveries, compares their sizes and orbits, and highlights those that fall in the habitable zone.

feat.edge: on the edge of the habitable zone

scatter.title: Size vs orbital period
scatter.legend: Teal — confirmed planets, gold — unconfirmed candidates (TOI)

notable.title: Exoplanets worth knowing
notable.f1_body: The closest known exoplanet to Earth, in the habitable zone around the star nearest to the Sun.
notable.f2_body: Seven Earth-sized planets around a single red dwarf, three of them in the habitable zone.
notable.f3_body: One of the first Earth-sized planets found in the habitable zone of a Sun-like star.

interact.hint: Click a planet in the TOI-700 system or a catalog row to see its details.
interact.clickRow: Click a row to expand details

blurb.habitable: Potentially habitable: equilibrium temperature in the 180–310 K range where liquid water could exist on the surface.
blurb.edge: On the edge of the habitable zone — temperature is near the limit for liquid water.
blurb.hot: Too hot for liquid water on the surface.
blurb.cold: Too cold for liquid water without a dense atmosphere.
blurb.unknown: No temperature data — habitability is undetermined.

trappist1.name: TRAPPIST-1
trappist1.caption: Seven Earth-sized planets around a cool red dwarf; three in the habitable zone. 39 ly.

toi700.name: TOI-700
toi700.caption: Four planets found by TESS; TOI-700 d is potentially habitable. 101 ly.

kepler1649.name: Kepler-1649
kepler1649.caption: Two planets in the habitable zone — one of the most Earth-like systems known. 301 ly.

proxima.name: Proxima Centauri
proxima.caption: The nearest star system; Proxima b is a habitability candidate. 4.2 ly.

kepler186.name: Kepler-186
kepler186.caption: Five planets; Kepler-186 f was the first Earth-sized planet found in the habitable zone. 564 ly.

k218.name: K2-18
k218.caption: A mini-Neptune in the habitable zone: water vapor was found in K2-18 b's atmosphere. 124 ly.

peg51.name: 51 Pegasi
peg51.caption: First planet found around a Sun-like star (1995) — hot Jupiter 51 Peg b in 4.2 days. 51 ly.

gj1214.name: GJ 1214
gj1214.caption: The best-studied mini-Neptune: GJ 1214 b with a hydrogen-helium atmosphere. 48 ly.

solar.name: Sun
solar.caption: Our Solar System — a scale reference, Mercury to Neptune.

hero.title: Deep space
hero.sub: Things beyond daily ISS passes: space debris around Earth, the most powerful explosions in the Universe — gamma-ray bursts, and the farthest human-made probes, Voyager, flying into interstellar space.

s1.title: Space debris
s1.sub: Everything orbiting Earth that is uncontrolled: spent upper stages, dead satellites, collision fragments. Even a 1-cm fragment at orbital velocity hits like a shell.

debris.whyBody: Debris breeds more debris: collisions cascade fragments (Kessler syndrome), threatening comms and navigation satellites.
debris.dataBody: Statistics from the ESA Space Environment Report {{year}}. Updated yearly.

s2.title: Gamma-ray bursts (GRB)
s2.sub: Gamma-ray bursts are the most energetic explosions in the Universe since the Big Bang: collapse of a massive star or merger of neutron stars. NASA GCN sends circulars to observers within minutes of an event.

grb.whatBody1: A GRB lasts from milliseconds to hundreds of seconds. In a few seconds it emits more energy than the Sun in a billion years. Light from the farthest GRBs takes billions of years to reach us.
grb.whatBody2: GCN (Gamma-ray Coordinates Network) relays the burst's coordinates to telescopes worldwide for rapid follow-up of the “afterglow”.

v1.title: Voyager 1 — the farthest human-made object
v1.body: Launched in 1977. The first probe in interstellar space (2017). Carries the “Golden Record” with a message for aliens.

v2.title: Voyager 2 — the only probe to visit all ice giants
v2.body: Flew past Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune. Also in interstellar space (2018). Its signal takes over 18 hours to reach Earth.

hero.eyebrow: 49 years in flight · beyond the Solar System
hero.title:
The path taken by
Voyager 1 and 2
hero.sub: Two spacecraft launched in the summer of 1977 became the farthest human-made objects in history. Both have left the heliosphere and are flying through interstellar space, moving tens of kilometers farther every second.

legend.v1: Voyager 1 — course north of the ecliptic
legend.v2: Voyager 2 — course south of the ecliptic
legend.helio: Heliopause — the limit of the Sun's influence

cards.lightHours: ≈ {{n}} h of light travel to Earth

tl.t1_body: Voyager 2 launched first but on a slower trajectory — Voyager 1 caught up and overtook it before Jupiter.
tl.t2_body: Both probes discovered active volcanoes on Io and a thin ring around Jupiter.
tl.t3_body: Detailed images of the ring system; after this the probes' paths parted for good.
tl.t4_body: The only close-up images of these planets in history — Voyager 1 took the northern route and visited neither.
tl.t5_body: Instruments recorded a sharp drop in solar-wind pressure — both probes officially entered interstellar space.
tl.t6_title: Farther than anything humanity has ever built
tl.t6_body: Both probes keep transmitting data about the interstellar medium, cosmic rays and magnetic fields.

rtg.v1Body: Power drops by about 4 W per year. At current estimates, the last science instruments will have to be shut down around 2025–2027, after which the probe will continue flying silently.
rtg.v2Body: NASA engineers have already turned off some heater systems to keep the science instruments running a few more years beyond the original plan.

gold.title: A message for anyone who finds them
gold.num: built to last billions of years
gold.body: Each probe carries a gold-plated copper record with sounds of Earth, music from many cultures and greetings in 55 languages — including Ukrainian.

orbitMap.helioLabel: HELIOPAUSE · edge of the Solar System
orbitMap.hint: Click a milestone dot to learn more

milestones.start: Voyager 2 launched Aug 20, 1977 — 16 days before Voyager 1 (Sep 5, 1977), but on a slower trajectory.
milestones.v1_jupiter: Jupiter flyby in March 1979 — first close-ups of the Great Red Spot and the planet's rings.
milestones.v1_saturn: Saturn flyby in November 1980 — studied the rings and the moon Titan.
milestones.v1_helio: Aug 25, 2012: Voyager 1 became the first craft to cross the heliopause, the solar wind's edge.
milestones.v1_now: The farthest human-made object — over 166 AU from the Sun, in interstellar space.
milestones.v2_jupiter: Jupiter flyby in July 1979 — discovered active volcanoes on the moon Io.
milestones.v2_saturn: Saturn flyby in August 1981 — trajectory bent onward toward Uranus.
milestones.v2_uranus: Uranus flyby in January 1986 — the only close-up images of the planet in history.
milestones.v2_neptune: Neptune flyby in August 1989 — the final planetary encounter, then heading south.
milestones.v2_helio: Nov 5, 2018: Voyager 2 crossed the heliopause, second after Voyager 1.
milestones.v2_now: Over 139 AU from the Sun, sending interstellar data from the southern direction.

hero.title:
RTL-SDR as a real
scientific instrument
hero.sub: For a cheap USB receiver and a simple antenna you can do real science — from receiving Jupiter's radio emission to counting meteors by radio reflection. The data you collect is used by professional observation networks too.

s1.title: Jupiter radio bursts
s1.sub: Jupiter is a powerful source of natural radio emission. The interaction of its magnetosphere with the moon Io creates short pulses (S-bursts) and longer “drifting” bursts (L-bursts) you can hear and see on an ordinary SDR receiver with a simple antenna — no telescope needed.

rtl.spectroCaption: Example waterfall of Jupiter reception, 18–28 MHz

cards.bestTimeFoot: Activity is tied to the position of the moon Io in its orbit
cards.networkFoot: A NASA project where amateurs submit their data

sBurst.title: S-bursts
sBurst.body: Very short pulses (milliseconds) that sound like crackling or “popcorn pops”. They arrive in bursts during active Io passes past specific magnetic field lines of Jupiter.

lBurst.title: L-bursts
lBurst.body: Last from seconds to a few minutes, with a slight frequency drift — they sound like surf or wind noise that rises and falls.

s2.title: Meteor radio monitoring
s2.sub: When a meteor burns up in the atmosphere it leaves a brief ionized trail that can reflect signals from distant transmitters — TV towers, radars or even foreign FM stations. An RTL-SDR pointed at such a distant signal (one normally not received directly due to Earth's curvature) registers short “pings” each time a meteor flies between transmitter and receiver.

rtl.pingCaption: Example recording: quiet background and five reflected “ping” signals from meteors

metCards.rangeFoot: VHF: distant FM/TV stations, GRAVES radars
metCards.measureFoot: Pings per hour — the basis of your own activity statistics
metCards.useFoot: Works during the day and in cloudy weather — unlike visual observing

rtl.showerBody: The number of recorded radio pings correlates well with a shower's peak — you can plot your own activity chart even under a cloudy sky.

gear.p1: A receiver with direct sampling for the HF band — that's what lets it receive Jupiter's 18–28 MHz without an extra converter.
gear.p2: A simple dipole or a magnetic loop antenna tuned to 20 MHz — even a basic build gives acceptable reception.
gear.p3: A directional antenna (Yagi) for your chosen distant transmitter significantly increases the number of recorded pings compared to a simple dipole.
gear.p4: SDR# or GNU Radio for reception and recording; dedicated RadioJOVE software for analyzing and submitting Jupiter observations to the NASA network.
gear.p5: Far from electrical noise sources (power supplies, Wi-Fi routers) — industrial interference often masks faint natural signals.

rtl.communityTitle: Discussing antennas, settings and reception results
rtl.communityBody: OrbitLight members share Jupiter spectrograms, compare meteor ping statistics and help with first SDR setup.

hero.title:
The sky is more interesting
when you look not alone
hero.sub: Forum, photo gallery, joint observations and personal diaries — a space for everyone who looks up. Join via Telegram in one click.

cats.c1_p: What you saw tonight, planning tips
cats.c2_p: Telescopes, binoculars, cameras — choice and maintenance

quiz.q: Which planet in the Solar System has the shortest day?

diaries.d1_text: Caught 6 Perseid meteors in an hour, though the peak isn't here yet. Sky 4 out of 5, slight light pollution from the city. Jupiter is especially sharp today in the 8" Dobson — all four Galilean moons visible.
diaries.d2_text: The ISS passed exactly as the bot predicted — appeared in the southwest, duration 4 min. Managed to film it on my phone, got a bright streak on a long-exposure shot.

meetups.m1_h: Perseid peak observing, T. Shevchenko park
meetups.m1_p: Sumy · 14 participants registered · bring a blanket and a thermos
meetups.m2_h: Joint viewing of the partial eclipse

hero.title: Satellites live
hero.sub: Thousands of objects in orbit — Starlink, ISS, bright and weather satellites — move on the map in real time. Click a marker to learn the object's altitude, velocity and coordinates.

satellites.s1_sub: Positions are calculated right in the browser from orbital elements (TLE, Celestrak) — with no per-frame API calls. Click markers for details. Data updates every second.

cards.tleBody: The server fetches two-line orbital elements from Celestrak and serves them to the browser via /api/tle.
cards.sgp4Body: The satellite.js library computes each satellite's position for the current moment using the SGP4 model.
cards.realtimeBody: Every second markers move to new coordinates — with no server calls. Click for altitude, velocity and coordinates.

notFound.title: Lost in space
notFound.body: This page doesn't exist in our galaxy. Perhaps a black hole swallowed it, or you simply drifted off course.
